stories.headline: Human Remains of Three Young Children Discovered in Memphis Woods
stories.summary:
The remains of three children, estimated to be between the ages of 3 and 7, have been found in a heavily wooded area in the Hickory Hill neighborhood of southwest Memphis, Tennessee. The investigation began March 8 after an anonymous caller reported finding a human skull near the woods. Cadaver dogs and a subsequent drainage system search led to the discovery of additional skulls and 14 bones consistent with human remains.

Memphis Police Chief Cerelyn Davis announced the findings at a press conference Wednesday, confirming that initial medical examiner analysis identified the remains as belonging to three children. Approximately 170 personnel, including FBI agents, are involved in the ongoing search of the crime scene. Investigators have not yet determined the identities of the children or the cause of death.

Authorities are urging anyone with information to contact police, noting that the case is deeply concerning to the community. Chief Davis stressed there is no indication of an active public threat but called on the public for help, saying: 'Somebody knows where these children have come from. Someone knows the missing children that we have discovered in this area.'

stories.headline: Medical Examiner Rules Death of Teen Found in Singer D4vd's Car a Homicide
stories.summary:
The Los Angeles County medical examiner's office has ruled the death of 14-year-old Celeste Rivas Hernandez a homicide, announcing Wednesday that she died of multiple penetrating injuries caused by an undisclosed object or objects. Her body was found in September in the front trunk of a Tesla registered to TikTok star and singer D4vd, whose real name is David Anthony Burke, 21. Stab wounds were found on her abdomen and chest during the autopsy, and shredded blue plastic was discovered in some of her wounds.

Prosecutors allege Rivas Hernandez was killed and her body dismembered on or around May 5, 2025, roughly four months before she was found. A toxicology report indicated the presence of methamphetamine or MDMA and low levels of alcohol, but noted neither appeared to play a role in her death. Burke has been charged with murder and pleaded not guilty; his attorneys say they will 'vigorously defend his innocence' and contend the evidence will show he did not kill her.

The medical examiner's office had determined the cause of death in December but was barred from releasing findings publicly during the police investigation. Chief Medical Examiner Dr. Odey Ukpo expressed frustration at the delay, calling it 'unfathomable' that the family had to wait so long. Burke is scheduled to appear in Los Angeles court Thursday morning.

stories.headline: Update: RFK Jr. Concludes Marathon Congressional Hearings, Deflects Blame for Measles Outbreaks
stories.summary:
Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. wrapped up a multiday series of congressional budget hearings Wednesday, during which he defended the Trump administration's proposed 2027 budget that would cut more than 12% of Department of Health and Human Services funding. Kennedy acknowledged the cuts were 'painful' but argued they were necessary given the federal government's $39 trillion deficit. Lawmakers from both parties raised concerns about reductions to research funding and public health programs.

A central point of contention was responsibility for declining childhood vaccination rates and ongoing measles outbreaks across the country. Kennedy repeatedly denied culpability, saying 'It has nothing to do with me,' and pointed to rising measles cases globally in countries including Canada, Mexico, and the United Kingdom. He disputed characterizations of him as anti-vaccine, saying he is 'pro-science,' though he spent years publicly questioning vaccine safety and in 2021 urged people to 'resist' CDC guidelines.

Democratic lawmakers accused Kennedy of spreading misinformation and causing a 'spillover effect' on public health trust, while Kennedy accused Democrats of grandstanding and seeking sound bites. Republicans largely backed his framing of HHS restructuring as fiscally necessary. Surveys cited during the hearings show trust in federal health agencies has continued to decline since Kennedy took office, a trend he attributed to erosion that began during the COVID-19 pandemic.

stories.headline: Nigeria's Suspected Coup Plotters Plead Not Guilty to Treason Charges
stories.summary:
Six people, including a retired major general and a serving police inspector, appeared in Nigeria's Federal High Court in Abuja on Wednesday and pleaded not guilty to charges of treason, terrorism, and money laundering for allegedly plotting to overthrow President Bola Tinubu. The defendants were ordered to remain in the custody of Nigeria's domestic intelligence agency, the Department of State Services. A seventh alleged conspirator, former petroleum minister and ex-governor Timipre Sylva, remains at large.

Rumors of the plot first surfaced in October 2025 when Nigeria abruptly canceled a military parade marking the country's 65th Independence Anniversary, with officials citing security concerns. In January, the military announced 16 officers would face a separate military tribunal. The civilians and retired military personnel now facing civilian court charges are accused of conspiring with a serving army colonel to 'levy war against the state to overawe' the president.

Defense lawyers complained during Wednesday's proceedings that they had been denied access to their clients since September 2025, suggesting the defendants may have been in custody for months before charges were formally filed. Prosecutors allege money changed hands linked to terrorism financing, with bail hearings scheduled for April 27. Nigeria has experienced multiple coups since independence, making the case a significant test of the country's democratic institutions.

stories.headline: Update: Billionaire Justin Sun Sues Trump Family's World Liberty Crypto Firm Over Alleged Token Seizure
stories.summary:
Billionaire crypto entrepreneur Justin Sun has filed a lawsuit in San Francisco federal court accusing World Liberty Financial, the crypto venture co-founded by President Donald Trump and his son Eric Trump, of freezing his digital tokens and threatening to destroy them without justification. Sun, who initially invested $45 million in the firm, alleges the company has blocked him from selling or voting his WLFI tokens — which at their peak were valued at more than $1 billion — in what he calls an 'illegal scheme.' The price of a single WLFI token has fallen from 31 cents to under 8 cents since September.

Sun alleges that co-founder Chase Herro and others are using the Trump brand to 'profit through fraud,' and that early promises allowing token holders to eventually trade their holdings were 'false and misleading.' He also purchased $100 million of Trump's meme coins in July 2025. World Liberty denied wrongdoing, with co-founder Zach Witkoff — son of Trump Middle East envoy Steve Witkoff — calling the suit a 'desperate attempt to deflect attention from Sun's own misconduct.' Eric Trump dismissed the lawsuit as 'ridiculous.'

The case adds to scrutiny surrounding the Trump family's cryptocurrency ventures and their intersection with foreign investors. The Securities and Exchange Commission separately dropped its own investigation into Sun earlier this year. The new development in this previously reported story is the formal filing of Sun's federal complaint, which adds specific legal allegations including details about the token freezing, vote-stripping, and threatened destruction of his holdings.
